title: RAIDEN FIGHTERS REMIX COLLECTION
anchor_summary: A classic arcade vertical shoot-'em-up compilation centered on the Raiden Fighters trilogy, emphasizing fast stage runs, ship variety, score-chasing, and remastered collection features.

similar_games.title: Raiden III x MIKADO MANIAX
similar_games.why_similar: Another Raiden-series vertical shmup built around dense bullet patterns, arcade-style runs, score attack, and modern re-release features.

similar_games.title: Raiden IV x MIKADO remix
similar_games.why_similar: Very close fit as a modernized Raiden release focused on classic vertical shooting, chaining survival and scoring, and arranged music options.

similar_games.title: Psikyo Shooting Stars Alpha
similar_games.why_similar: A recognized retro shmup collection with multiple arcade-style vertical shooters, short replayable runs, and leaderboard-driven mastery.

similar_games.title: Capcom Arcade Stadium
similar_games.why_similar: Broader compilation, but similarly packages classic arcade shooters and other score-attack games with modern collection conveniences.

similar_games.title: DoDonPachi Resurrection
similar_games.why_similar: A highly recognizable vertical bullet-hell shooter with intense stage play, ship/shot style selection, and deep scoring appeal for arcade shmup fans.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies this as a compilation of the Raiden Fighters arcade shooting games, emphasizing pixel graphics, high-tempo action, scoring systems, and online rankings. That strongly indicates a classic arcade shoot-'em-up/bullet-hell style collection. However, that gameplay identity does not map cleanly to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype in the provided list, and forcing it into an unrelated archetype would be inaccurate.

title: Afterplace
anchor_summary: Open-world retro action-adventure RPG centered on getting lost: top-down exploration, hidden paths and dungeons, light hack-and-slash combat, quirky NPC dialogue, minimal handholding, and choice-shaped endings.

similar_games.title: TUNIC
similar_games.why_similar: Top-down exploration-first adventure with cryptic world discovery, hidden routes, light action combat, and very little handholding.

similar_games.title: Hyper Light Drifter
similar_games.why_similar: Retro-styled top-down action adventure built around open-ended roaming, secret hunting, and fast combat in a mysterious world.

similar_games.title: UNSIGHTED
similar_games.why_similar: Open-ended top-down action RPG with dungeon-like spaces, upgrade progression, NPC interactions, and flexible route choice.

similar_games.title: Death's Door
similar_games.why_similar: Compact action-adventure focused on exploration, secrets, and responsive melee combat, though it is more structured than Afterplace.

similar_games.title: The Legend of Zelda: Link's Awakening
similar_games.why_similar: Classic adventure template for roaming, talking to odd characters, finding hidden paths, clearing dungeons, and upgrading through discovery.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe Afterplace as a single-player open-world adventure focused on free exploration, hidden paths, secrets, dungeons, monster fighting, NPC conversations, and multiple endings driven by player choices. The strongest identity signals are open-world exploration, handcrafted discovery, quest/exploration progression, branching narrative outcomes, and active melee-oriented combat against monsters. RPG elements like weapon upgrades, armor purchases, and choice-driven endings support a secondary western_narrative_rpg read, but the dominant presentation in the evidence is open-world action-adventure rather than a deeper stat- or party-driven RPG. Perspective is not explicitly stated in the stored evidence, but the remaining evidence is strong enough to support the archetype.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Top-down exploration-first adventure with cryptic world discovery, hidden routes, light action combat, and very little handholding.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently frames the game around a non-combat, chapter-based mystery investigation: revisiting a decades-old murder, gathering clues, interviewing suspects, and uncovering the truth. Metacritic explicitly labels it as a Visual Novel, while the description emphasizes authored story progression, detective focus, and no combat-driven loop. The evidence for hidden-object gameplay is weaker because clue-finding is mentioned, but explicit hidden-object scenes or object-list puzzle structure are not. Overall the strongest fit is visual_novel with detective-mystery framing.

title: Tales of Berseria Remastered
anchor_summary: A remastered party-based action JRPG centered on real-time combo combat, Artes customization, controllable party members, and a story-driven campaign with series-typical Tales structure.

similar_games.title: Tales of Berseria
similar_games.why_similar: The original release shares the same core campaign, party system, real-time Artes combat, progression, and world structure.

similar_games.title: Tales of Arise
similar_games.why_similar: Another modern Tales action JRPG with real-time party combat, Artes chaining, character swapping, and a story-led adventure structure.

similar_games.title: Tales of Zestiria
similar_games.why_similar: Very close series neighbor with the same Tales formula of real-time battles, party management, overworld exploration, and story-driven progression.

similar_games.title: Tales of Vesperia: Definitive Edition
similar_games.why_similar: Shares the recognizable Tales loop of party-based action combat, combo-heavy Artes use, skits, towns/dungeons, and long-form JRPG progression.

similar_games.title: Scarlet Nexus
similar_games.why_similar: A party-oriented action RPG with fast combo combat, ability-driven chaining, character bonds, and a strongly narrative campaign.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and metadata evidence consistently describe a story-driven action RPG centered on Velvet and her companions, indicating a campaign-focused JRPG structure rather than a loot or open-world identity. The description explicitly emphasizes a revenge journey across Midgand, a cast of companions, authored story themes, destination markers, and a combat system built around choosing Artes, performing combos, and collecting Souls for attack barrages. Stored signals also strongly support party-based play: party_tactics, party_management, party_control, and party_management_loop all appear in the audit payload with relatively high confidence. Metacritic labels it Action RPG, while Steam categories add single-player and co-op support, which fits Tales-style auxiliary co-op without displacing the core single-player campaign identity. Some facets like exact topology and perspective are inferred from the remaster/store framing and franchise-typical presentation cues in the text rather than being directly spelled out, so confidence is solid but not maximal.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: The original release shares the same core campaign, party system, real-time Artes combat, progression, and world structure.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Towerborne as a side-scrolling 'Action RPG Brawler' with classic combo-based combat, four weapon styles, fast responsive encounters, and up-to-4-player co-op. The loop is hub-to-expedition-to-return: venture out from the Belfry, fight through enemies, then return to turn in quests, reforge gear, and customize builds. That supports beat_em_up as the core gameplay identity, with secondary loot_action_rpg elements from gear, crafting resources, build flexibility, and progression. The current computed taxonomy appears noisy in places, but the underlying store text strongly supports a co-op side-scrolling brawler rather than platformer or traditional fighter.

title: Utawarerumono: ZAN 2
anchor_summary: Story-driven anime-style hack-and-slash action RPG built around mission-based progression, a large roster of playable characters, party management, character growth systems, and optional co-op/arena-style play. The clearest editorial comps are musou-adjacent action RPGs with heavier story and team-building emphasis rather than pure one-character character action or traditional JRPGs.

similar_games.why_similar: Party-based action RPG that mixes musou-style battles with story progression, team management, and character growth between missions.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 3D hack-and-slash action RPG centered on fast-paced combat, a 20+ character roster, and 50+ story missions. The Steam detailed description gives the strongest identity signals: '3D HACK-AND-SLASH REBORN,' 'mix and match flashy combat techniques,' 'evolvable abilities,' and 'create an unstoppable team' all support a character-action core with party-management elements rather than a pure solo combo-action game. The presence of a primary story mode following Mask of Truth, a base system for interacting with companions and strengthening characters, and an online/single-player Dream Arena side mode supports a campaign-driven, mission-based structure with secondary JRPG-story-RPG traits. Evidence is strong enough to accept, but not strong enough to place it in a more specific action-RPG subtype from the allowed list.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Direct predecessor with the same core mission-based anime hack-and-slash structure, playable roster, and story adaptation format.

Metacritic explicitly calls it a 'side-scrolling precision platformer' in retro pixel art style, which strongly supports precision_platformer as the core identity. The game appears level-based across 5 themed worlds with handcrafted challenges and traversal-focused play. Enemy presence and 'defeat this nightmare' language indicate light action elements, making action_platformer a reasonable secondary archetype, but the dominant loop is precise movement and obstacle navigation rather than combat.

title: Dice A Million
anchor_summary: A single-player roguelike deckbuilder/bagbuilder built around dice, passive item synergies, run-based progression, score/debt thresholds, and boss fights, with a strong 'break the game' combo-building feel.

similar_games.why_similar: Run-based dice combat where you build around class-specific loadouts, dice effects, and synergistic equipment.

similar_games.why_similar: A true dice-driven roguelike deckbuilder focused on assembling pools of effect dice and chaining powerful synergies through runs.

similar_games.title: SpellRogue
similar_games.why_similar: Dice-powered roguelike run structure with spell/item builds, tactical roll management, and escalating encounters.

similar_games.title: Slice & Dice
similar_games.why_similar: Turn-based roguelike centered on dice outcomes, party build decisions, and repeated runs driven by optimization and adaptation.

similar_games.title: Luck be a Landlord
similar_games.why_similar: Combo-heavy roguelike about pushing luck, clearing debt thresholds, and breaking scoring systems with escalating synergies.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently identifies Dice A Million as a 'roguelike deckbuilder' built around randomly generated runs, combining 120+ dice for synergies, equipping passive rings, choosing among multiple hands/classes, and overcoming escalating debts and bosses. That strongly matches a run-based deck-construction game with buildcraft, metaprogression/unlocks, tactical optimization, and boss encounters. Although the theme is dice rather than literal cards, the core gameplay identity is clearly deckbuilder/card-battler adjacent, and card_battler is the closest supported archetype in the taxonomy.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Run-based dice combat where you build around class-specific loadouts, dice effects, and synergistic equipment.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as a Reigns-style swipe-driven narrative/choice game: 'swipe right, swipe left,' 'thousands of storyline combinations,' 'navigate the moral maze,' puzzle-solving, unlocking deeds/characters, and leveling up Dandelion's career. That is strong evidence for a branching choice-centric design, but the allowed archetype set does not include a clear Reigns/interactive-choice/narrative-card archetype. Available action RPG or beat 'em up candidates in the current taxonomy debug payload are contradicted by the actual text, which does not support third-person melee action as the core gameplay loop. Because the gameplay identity is clear yet does not map cleanly and safely to an allowed archetype, rejection is the most accurate outcome.

title: City Hunter
anchor_summary: Available evidence is sparse, but this appears to be a revival/remake of SUNSOFT’s 1990 City Hunter game: a single-player retro action-adventure built around crime-fighting jobs, urban stages, and likely side-scrolling run-and-gun/action-platforming structure rather than modern open-world play.

similar_games.title: Rolling Thunder 2
similar_games.why_similar: Classic side-scrolling secret-agent action with deliberate gunplay, enemy-heavy stages, and arcade-style mission progression.

similar_games.why_similar: Urban mission-based arcade action focused on shooting through compact stages with a cinematic crime-thriller feel.

similar_games.title: Vice: Project Doom
similar_games.why_similar: Story-led retro action that mixes side-scrolling gun combat and platforming with a police/crime-fighting structure.

similar_games.title: Huntdown
similar_games.why_similar: Modernized side-scrolling bounty-hunter shooter with stage-based progression, urban targets, and heavy emphasis on gunplay.

similar_games.title: Code Name: Viper
similar_games.why_similar: NES-era action platform shooter centered on infiltrating enemy-filled stages and rescuing targets under constant fire.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes only that this is a single-player action/adventure remake tied to the City Hunter license. The descriptions are almost entirely promotional and story-focused, with no concrete gameplay information about perspective, traversal, combat loop, mission structure, world topology, or progression. Because the gameplay identity is not described with enough specificity to distinguish among multiple action-adventure archetypes, the evidence is too thin for a reliable taxonomy mapping.

That strongly supports precision_platformer over broader platformer archetypes: the core loop is stage clearing through difficult platforming challenges rather than combat, exploration, or collection. Evidence supports level-based progression, platform-navigation mechanics, score-attack replayability, mastery-heavy input demands, and no meaningful combat emphasis.

title: Supernova
anchor_summary: A casual single-player Suika-style merging puzzle game: you drop/combine similarly sized planets in a container to form larger ones and chase high scores.

similar_games.title: Tetris Effect: Connected

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is materially contradictory. Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently present Supernova as a space-themed Suika-style matching puzzle focused on combining planets for high score, while the OpenCritic description instead describes a sci-fi MOBA/strategy game with upgradeable armies, commanders, and crafting from drops. These imply fundamentally different gameplay identities, and neither line of evidence is strong enough to cleanly map into a supported Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype with confidence. Because the bundle appears to conflate different titles or contains unreliable metadata, classification should be rejected.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: The clearest match: physics-based drop-and-merge puzzle play where identical objects combine into larger ones for score optimization.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Royal Vermin as a 2D local platform fighter for 2-4 players, explicitly 'inspired by the best platform fighters.' The core loop is match-based PvP: players climb, throw, use ranged chargeable projectiles, knock opponents out of collapsing arenas, and aim to win rounds in fast, chaotic matches. Side-scrolling presentation, duel-focused combat, dominant combat presence, and win-match goals strongly support traditional_fighter over platformer; platforming is present but serves the fighting format rather than precision challenge progression. Party-game qualities are notable through local same-screen multiplayer and chaotic comeback-focused design, but they are secondary to the fighter identity.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: The clearest comp: multiplayer platform-fighter matches focused on knockbacks, ring-outs, stage hazards, and easy-to-learn party play.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies SealChain as a single-player roguelike centered on item-linking, formations/seals, random events, character builds, talent trees, and combat against monsters. However, the available text does not establish a strong fit to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not clearly a card battler despite combinatorial item synergies, not clearly turn-based tactics despite strategy language, and not supportable as co_op_action_roguelite because the evidence indicates single-player only. The Metacritic 'Top-Down Shoot-'Em-Up' label is too weak and isolated to anchor a shooter archetype. Overall the gameplay identity is real but falls between the available archetypes, so classification should be rejected rather than forced.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Stillborn Slayer as a single-player dark fantasy action roguelike with fast hack-and-slash combat, procedural runs, brutal difficulty, boss fights, dodging/rolling, and a grim horror-inflected tone. However, the evidence does not firmly establish a supported taxonomy archetype from the allowed list. The strongest identity is run-based action roguelite, but the available archetype list only includes co_op_action_roguelite, which contradicts the explicitly single-player framing. Alternative fits such as soulslike_action_rpg or character_action remain too weak because perspective is not established, progression is described as run/repetition-based rather than RPG progression, and the copy also emphasizes bullet-hell roguelike structure over a stable campaign RPG format. Because the title's core gameplay identity appears to fall between unsupported/insufficiently evidenced archetypes, classification should be rejected for now.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 30+ hour JRPG with a party-based adventure, explorable isometric dungeons, a world map linking towns and quests, and explicit turn-based battles where the player chooses each party member's actions and plans turns tactically. The text also emphasizes non-linear progression after the prologue, several branching endings, relationship/trust systems with party members and towns, and equipment/resource loops for commissioning gear. That combination strongly supports jrpg_story_rpg as the core identity, with some tactical overlap from its turn-based party combat.

similar_games.title: Ys X: Nordics

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently describes Ys X: Proud Nordics as the definitive edition of an action RPG adventure centered on fast-paced combat, exploration of a large island/map area, boss fights, and an authored story campaign. The copy emphasizes 'fast-paced combat,' 'Cross Action,' 'Mana Action,' new bosses, a high-difficulty dungeon, and broad map/exploration content, all of which support a single-player fantasy action-RPG identity. The current computed taxonomy pointing to traditional_fighter is contradicted by the actual descriptive text, which clearly frames a narrative-driven exploratory action RPG rather than a PvP or match-based fighter. The strongest fit in the available taxonomy is open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, though the world structure reads more semi-open than fully open world.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Proud Nordics is the enhanced definitive edition of the original, retaining the same core combat, exploration, progression, and story foundation.

title: PancitoMerge
anchor_summary: A cozy single-player drop-and-merge puzzle game in the Suika mold: you place falling items into a container, combine matching pieces into higher-tier ones, avoid topping out, and chase high scores with a light twist from periodic customer requests.

similar_games.title: Suika Game
similar_games.why_similar: Nearly the same core loop: drop physics-driven objects into a container, merge identical pieces into larger ones, avoid overflow, and push for high scores.

similar_games.title: Triple Town
similar_games.why_similar: Also built around chaining identical pieces into higher-tier items under space pressure, with score optimization and run-ending board management.

similar_games.title: Puyo Puyo Champions
similar_games.why_similar: Shares the falling-piece puzzle structure, quick-session replayability, and topping-out failure state, though it focuses on match chains rather than merge evolution.

similar_games.title: Tetris Effect: Connected
similar_games.why_similar: A recognizable falling-block comparison for its stack management, score chase, and simple-to-learn arcade puzzle flow, even if its matching rules differ.
A famous merge-up progression comp: combine identical pieces into bigger ones while preserving space and planning several moves ahead.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly indicates a casual single-player merge/falling-block style puzzle game focused on dropping, stacking, and merging breads for score, with light customer-request interactions and leaderboard play. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not include a general merge puzzle, falling-block puzzle, or score-attack puzzle category. The computed candidate of hidden_object_puzzle is not supported by the described gameplay loop, which centers on spatial dropping/merging rather than hidden-object searching. Because the game's identity is clear but does not cleanly fit any allowed archetype, it should be rejected rather than force-mapped.
